refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, visibility, and adaptive control. This supports both Site Manager acceptance criteria and Operator/Tele-operator decision-making.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = (total_energy_consumed_kWh / total_tonnage_moved) over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 1 week of representative operations). Compare against E_target. Verify that measurement system is calibrated and that feedback loops are functional.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: Monitor and report real-time energy consumption (kWh) and work output (tonnage moved) to enable efficiency calculation.
CR-NGHE-003.2: Provide operators and tele-operators with visibility into current and historical energy_per_ton performance against E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.3: Enable control strategies (e.g., load optimization, route planning, idle reduction) that reduce energy_per_ton below E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.4: Ensure energy efficiency monitoring and control do not compromise safety criticality or operational safety policies.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized energy management system with real-time telemetry aggregation, efficiency KPI calculation, and operator feedback dashboard; coupled with vehicle-level power optimization (idle shutdown, load balancing).
DP-B: Distributed energy monitoring at vehicle level with local efficiency calculation and autonomous control; tele-operator receives summary metrics only.

DP-C: Hybrid approach: vehicle-level energy monitoring feeds into a central analytics engine that computes energy_per_ton, provides feedback via Telemetry interface, and recommends (but does not enforce) control actions to operators.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid energy efficiency architecture with vehicle-level monitoring, centralized analytics, and operator-guided optimization. Vehicle sensors measure power draw and work output; central system aggregates data via Telemetry interface, calculates energy_per_ton KPI, and communicates recommendations and alerts to operators and tele-operators. Control actions (load adjustment, route changes, idle management) remain operator-directed to preserve safety authority and situational awareness.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle power management and central analytics: mitigated by standardized Telemetry interface.
Coupling between operator feedback and safety-critical control: mitigated by explicit operator-in-the-loop design; safety alerts are separate from efficiency recommendations.
Coupling between energy optimization and worksite safety policies: mitigated by Safety Services module oversight and SafetyAlert interface.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids tight coupling by preserving operator authority and using well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert). Decoupling is achieved through asynchronous feedback loops and clear separation of concerns (monitoring vs. control vs. safety).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Operators and tele-operators will act on efficiency recommendations if they do not conflict with safety or task completion.
Assumption: Vehicle sensors (power, load, position) are available and calibrated.
Risk: If feedback latency is high (>5 min), operators may not perceive real-time efficiency impact and may not adjust behavior.
Risk: If energy_per_ton calculation is opaque or not trusted, operators may ignore recommendations.
Risk: Safety-critical control (e.g., emergency stop, load limit) must not be subordinated to efficiency optimization.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify in DP-C that safety-critical control actions (e.g., load limits, emergency procedures) are enforced independently of efficiency optimization. Efficiency recommendations are advisory and subject to operator override. Add explicit requirement that Telemetry feedback latency shall be ≤ 2 seconds to enable responsive operator behavior.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration module responsible for orchestrating energy efficiency KPI achievement across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Owns the central analytics engine, energy_per_ton calculation, operator feedback dashboard, and coordination of efficiency recommendations with safety constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional: Vehicle sends real-time power consumption, work output (tonnage), and operational state; NGHE Solution sends efficiency metrics, recommendations, and performance summaries back to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives task assignments (work orders, routes, load targets) from Site Manager or planning system; uses task context to inform efficiency optimization strategies.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives safety alerts from Safety Services and Vehicle; sends efficiency-related alerts (e.g., 'energy budget exceeded') that do not override safety constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution communicates with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via Network Link; ensures reliable delivery of telemetry, recommendations, and alerts.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of system-level energy efficiency orchestration, KPI calculation, and cross-module coordination. Minimal diffusion: analytics engine is centralized; interfaces are well-defined.

Vehicle could own local energy monitoring and control, but system-level KPI calculation and operator feedback require visibility across multiple vehicles and tasks. Assigning to Vehicle alone would diffuse efficiency strategy and complicate multi-vehicle coordination.

Teleoperation could own operator feedback and recommendation delivery, but lacks direct access to vehicle telemetry and safety constraints. Would require tight coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Safety Services owns safety-critical constraints but should not own efficiency optimization logic. Assigning energy efficiency to Safety Services would conflate safety and performance concerns, risking safety compromise.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because it has system-level visibility into all vehicles, tasks, and safety constraints. It can aggregate telemetry, calculate energy_per_ton KPI, and coordinate recommendations across operators and tele-operators without diffusing logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services modules.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic analytics engine, it may become a bottleneck for real-time feedback. Mitigation: Use asynchronous message queues and caching to decouple analytics from telemetry ingestion.

Risk: If NGHE Solution does not respect safety constraints from Safety Services, efficiency recommendations may inadvertently encourage unsafe behavior. Mitigation: Explicit integration with SafetyAlert interface; efficiency recommendations are filtered against active safety constraints before delivery to operators.

Risk: If Vehicle module does not provide reliable telemetry, NGHE Solution cannot calculate accurate energy_per_ton. Mitigation: Define telemetry SLA (availability, latency, accuracy) in Vehicle-NGHE interface contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts: (1) Vehicle → NGHE Solution: Telemetry (power, load, position, state) with ≤2s latency and ±5% accuracy. (2) NGHE Solution → Teleoperation: Efficiency metrics and recommendations via Telemetry interface. (3) Safety Services ↔ NGHE Solution: SafetyAlert interface for constraint synchronization. (4) Define NGHE Solution internal architecture: separate telemetry ingestion, analytics, and feedback delivery to avoid bottlenecks.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid energy efficiency architecture) directly addresses the refined FR by enabling real-time monitoring, centralized KPI calculation, and operator-guided optimization. The DP preserves operator authority and safety constraints while providing the feedback mechanisms necessary for energy_per_ton achievement. No decoupling or independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If analytics logic is split between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, efficiency calculation becomes inconsistent. Mitigation: Centralize analytics in NGHE Solution; Vehicle provides only raw telemetry.

Diffusion risk: If operator feedback is delivered via multiple channels (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert), operators may miss or misinterpret recommendations. Mitigation: Standardize feedback delivery via Telemetry interface with clear priority levels.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution containment is strong (95/100). Interfaces are well-defined and leverage existing known interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No new module boundaries required. Ownership is clear: NGHE Solution owns system-level energy efficiency orchestration.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: measure energy_per_ton over agreed evaluation period using telemetry from Vehicle module. Compare against E_target. Verify that feedback loops are functional and that operators receive timely recommendations. Acceptance criteria are measurable and traceable to IM boundaries.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns energy efficiency KPI achievement; Vehicle owns telemetry provision; Teleoperation owns operator feedback delivery; Safety Services owns safety constraint enforcement. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to explicitly state that efficiency recommendations are advisory and subject to operator override; safety-critical controls are enforced independently.
Define telemetry SLA in Vehicle-NGHE interface: latency ≤2s, accuracy ±5%, availability ≥99%.
Establish SafetyAlert filtering logic: efficiency recommendations are suppressed if they conflict with active safety constraints.
Add performance requirement: energy_per_ton feedback latency ≤5s to enable responsive operator behavior.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs to address monitoring, visibility, control, and safety integration separately. Each sub-FR will have distinct DPs and IMs. Proceeding to the next level will clarify the detailed architecture and enable more precise interface definitions and ownership assignments.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: Monitor and report real-time energy consumption (kWh) and work output (tonnage moved) to enable efficiency calculation.
CR-NGHE-003.2: Provide operators and tele-operators with visibility into current and historical energy_per_ton performance against E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.3: Enable control strategies (e.g., load optimization, route planning, idle reduction) that reduce energy_per_ton below E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.4: Ensure energy efficiency monitoring and control do not compromise safety criticality or operational safety policies.

assumptions: Vehicle module provides reliable telemetry (power, load, position, state) with ≤2s latency.
Operators and tele-operators will act on efficiency recommendations if they do not conflict with safety or task completion.
Energy_per_ton KPI is calculated as total_energy_consumed_kWh / total_tonnage_moved over agreed evaluation period.
Safety Services module enforces safety constraints independently of efficiency optimization.
Network Link provides reliable communication between NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
open_risks: Risk: Feedback latency >5s may reduce operator responsiveness to efficiency recommendations.
Risk: If energy_per_ton calculation is opaque, operators may not trust recommendations.
Risk: Efficiency optimization may inadvertently encourage unsafe behavior if not properly constrained by Safety Services.
Risk: Multi-vehicle coordination complexity may increase if energy_per_ton is calculated per-vehicle vs. per-fleet.
Risk: High safety criticality (per risk tailoring factors) requires explicit safety integration; any efficiency control that affects safety-critical systems must be validated.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: data types, latency, accuracy, availability SLAs.
Energy_per_ton calculation algorithm and validation methodology.
Operator feedback UI/UX design and usability testing results.
Safety constraint filtering logic and safety case integration.
Performance testing: feedback latency, analytics throughput, multi-vehicle scalability.
Acceptance test plan: energy_per_ton measurement methodology, evaluation period definition, E_target validation.
